profile.behavior: Aerial insectivore; catches flying insects in agile, low, swooping flight with quick turns and little gliding.
field_marks: Slender, streamlined swallow with very long, narrow, pointed wings.
Adult tail is long and deeply forked, with elongated outer streamers extending beyond the wingtips.
Steely blue to blue-black crown, back, wings, and tail.
Cinnamon to chestnut forehead and throat contrast with the dark blue face.
Underparts range from tawny or buff to pale; small white spots beneath the tail may show in flight.
Short, dark, broad-based bill and small dark legs.
profile.habitat: Open and semi-open country, including fields, grasslands, marshes, ponds, and coastal water; commonly forages over open ground or water and nests on sheltered human-made structures such as eaves, buildings, and bridges.

sources.title: Barn Swallow Identification — All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology

findings: Live verification confirms Barn Swallow (Hirundo rustica), family Hirundinidae. Cornell gives 15–19 cm length, 17–20 g weight, and 29–32 cm wingspan; the plate reproduces these correctly. Audubon independently gives 15–20 cm and describes the long forked tail, steel-blue back, chestnut throat, pale-to-buff underparts, and long narrow pointed wings.

The illustrated adult matches the references: steely blue upperparts, chestnut forehead/throat, buff underparts, short dark bill, pointed wings, and a deeply forked tail with elongated streamers. It shows one head, one beak, two wings in a side-on pose, two legs/feet, and one tail; no extra or malformed anatomy is apparent.
All visible text claims are consistent with the verified identification, behavior, habitat, and field marks. The plate contains no place name, ZIP code, coordinates, map, or local-observation detail.

Minor illustration caveat: the eye is slightly enlarged/stylized and the tail streamers are especially prominent, but both remain anatomically plausible for an adult Barn Swallow and are not material errors.

verification_sources.title: Barn Swallow Identification — All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology
